content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Lance H. Olson 
Olson Hagel & Fishburn 
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-255 
Dear Mr. Olson: 
January 20, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the disclosure and conflict of 
interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts 
presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of 
fact when rendering advice. (In re Oglesby (1975), 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
Under the Act, is the Six Basins Watennaster (the "Watennaster") a public agency required 
to adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code? 
CONCLUSION 
Based on the new infonnation you have provided, we are unable to conclude that the 
Watennaster is a public agency under the Act, as discussed below. 
FACTS 
On behalf the Pomona Valley Protective Association ("PVPA''), you request that we 
reconsider our advice and conclusion in the Adams Advice Letter, No. A-1 5-079.2 There, we 
examined the Watermaster utilizing the Siegel test and concluded that it was a public agency subject 
to the conflict of interest code requirements of the Act. 
PVPA is a member of the Watennaster, which was established by a final judgment (the 
"Judgment") entered in Southern California Water Company vs. City of la Verne, et al, a lawsuit 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are. to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
\ Because you ask that we reconsider the conclusion in the Adams Advice Letter, supra, we incorporate herein 
the facts and analysis from that letter. 


File No. A-16-255 
Page No. 2 
initiated in 1998 concerning groundwater adjudication of the hydrologic unit known as the Six 
Basins in eastern Los Angeles County. Following a period of extensive negotiation among the 
parties, the litigation was resolved pursuant to a stipulation among the public agencies and private 
litigants to allocate most of the groundwater in the defined area among the parties. 
It is customary in California groundwater adjudications to establish a Watermaster to 
administer and enforce a physical solution, an equitable remedy designed to implement the 
constitutional mandate and to maximize the beneficial use of water, subject to the ongoing 
supervision and jurisdiction of the Court. The Watermaster emanates from and enforces the judicial 
decree apportioning water among the parties. It assumes certain enumerated duties to implement the 
physical solution. 
The Judgment established a physical solution for the Six Basins Area and the Court 
expressly reserved jurisdiction over the implementation of the physical solution to address future or 
changed conditions. The Watermaster has a committee comprised ofrepresentatives from public 
agencies and private companies. "Governance responsibility was shared among these 
representatives on the committee in accordance with their weighted voting." 
ANALYSIS 
The Act prohibits a public official from making or participating in making a governmental 
decision in which the official knows or has reason to know he or she has a financial interest. 
(Section 87100.) The conflict of interest provisions of the Act apply only to "public officials." A 
"public official" is defined as every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local 
government agency. (Section 82048.) In addition, Section 87300 of the Act states that "[e]very 
agency shall adopt and promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code" applicable to its "designated 
employees." For the purposes of the Act, "agency" is interpreted to mean any state agency or local 
government agency. (Section 82003; Maas Advice Letter, No. A-98-261 .) 
As stated in the Adams Advice Letter, supra, the Commission established criteria for 
determining whether an entity is governmental in character in its opinion In re Siegel ( 1977) 3 
FPPC Ops. 62. The Siegel factors determine whether local entities are public or private in character 
for purposes of the Act.3 The Commission has applied the following four-part test: 
( 1) Whether the impetus for formation of the entity originated with a 
government agency; 
(2) Whether the entity is substantially funded by, or its primary source of 
funds is, a government agency; 
l The Siegel opinion concerns a local government agency. However, the Commission has applied what has 
become known as the "Siegel test" to identify state agencies as well. (Moser Advice Letter, No. A-97-400a citing In re 
Vonk (1981) 6 FPPC Ops. l.) Since all governmental agencies (state and local) are obligated under Section 87300 to 
adopt a conflict of interest code, there is no need to preserve distinctions between state and local agencies and, in fact, 
the fundamental purpose of the "Siegel test" is to distinguish governmental from non-governmental agencies, without 
regard to more incidental characteristics. 


File No. A-16-255 
Page No. 3 
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the entity was 
formed is to provide services or undertake obligations which public 
agencies are legally authorized to perform and which, in fact, they 
traditionally have performed; and 
(4) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other laws. 
In Siegel, the Commission found that, first, the Pico Rivera City Council was intimately 
involved in the formation of the Pico Rivera Water Development Corporation, and that the City 
Council had the right to disapprove the name of anyone submitted to serve on the board. Second, 
with respect to funding, the City was required to pay rent to the corporation until the corporation's 
bonds were retired, even ifreceipts from the operation of the water system were not sufficient to 
meet these costs - in essence, guaranteeing the bonds. Third, there was more evidence that the 
corporation was fulfilling a public function because the water system was to he operated solely by 
City employees. Further, the opinion considered it significant that the acquisition and operation of a 
water system is a service commonly provided by municipalities in their public capacities. Fourth, 
the corporation's bonds enjoyed the same legal status as those issued by a public body under 
California's tax and securities laws. Accordingly, the Commission concluded that the Pico Rivera 
Water Development Corporation was intrinsically "public" in character and therefore subject to the 
Act. 
We apply the Siegel test to your situation taking into consideration the additional 
background information you have provided to determine whether the Watermaster should be 
considered a public agency under the Act. 
l. Did the impetus for.formation of tlie entity originate wit/z a government entity? 
Generally, the first criterion is met where an entity is created by statute or ordnance or by 
some official action of another government agency. (Maas Advice Letter, No. A-98-261; Moser 
Advice Letter, No. A-97-400a.) 
In the Adams Advice Letter, we stated that although the Watermaster was created by the 
court Judgment, such creation "does not in itself mean that the impetus for formation of the entity 
originated with a government entity." In finding the first factor was satisfied, however, we 
considered the fact that parties in the litigation and signatories to the stipulated Judgment included 
the Cities of La Verne, Claremont, Pomona, and Upland. 
You acknowledge that the case involves a mix of public and private parties, but argue that 
the impetus for the formation of the Watermaster under the Judgment was the commencement of 
litigation by Southern California Water Company, a private entity. You further argue that only 
California courts, not public entities, can determine or adjudicate title to groundwater in California 
so the impetus did not originate with a public agency. And mere involvement by public agencies in 
this litigation does not bestow public agency status upon the Watermaster. 
You then distinguish the current matter from the situation in Siegel by noting that the 
Commission concluded the Pico Rivera City Council was intimately involved in the corporation's 
creation and it would not have come into existence but for the City's future planning. In addition, 


File No. A-16-255 
Page No. 4 
the City Council had absolute control over the corporation by virtue of its ability to "disapprove" 
the appointment of any director to the board. Lastly, the creation of the nonprofit corporation, 
which was really just an alter ego of the City, was simply a convenient method to obtain financing 
for a public works project. 
There is no dispute that the Watermaster was created by the Judgment in the underlying 
matter as we initially concluded. We do, however, find some merit in your position that the impetus 
for creation of the Watermaster did not necessarily originate with a government entity. Although 
government entities were involved in the underlying litigation, we find it significant that the lawsuit 
was initiated by Southern California Water Company, a private entity. Indeed, the necessity for the 
Watermaster is attributable to and arose directly from the filing of the lawsuit by this entity. And 
the presence of government entities in the lawsuit, although relevant, was not a necessary factor in 
the ultimate formation of the Watermaster (which can be created in cases involving only private 
litigants), and thus not determinative of the impetus of its creation. 
Accordingly, in light of these further considerations, we are unable to conclude that the 
impetus for creation of the Watermaster originated with a governmental entity.4 
2. Is the entity substalllial(v fimded by, or is its primmy source of fimds, a government agency? 
In the Adams Advice Letter, we noted that the Watermaster levies annual assessments to 
fund its work. We further noted that the parties to the Judgment are assessed in proportion to their 
assigned water rights, thus some of the funds are public funds. The letter cited to reports from 2014 
and 2015 concerning assessment amounts for major and minor parties, and concluded this Siegel 
factor was met because the "Watermaster is funded by member contributions, including substantial 
funding by members that are government agencies." 
You counter that the funds are not the result of taxes or fees levied generally by the 
govenunent and that "[m]uch of the Watermaster's funding derives from private entities." As an 
example, you state that about 47% of the shares of PVPA, are owned by Golden State Water 
Company, a non-public entity. You further claim that non-public funding from PVP A, not funding 
from public agencies, is a substantial source of funds to the Watermaster. You conclude that "the 
majority of funding to the Six Basins Watermaster originates from non-public sources." 
We believe our original conclusion that governmental agencies provide substantial funding 
through member contributions to the Watermaster was correct given the facts that were presented to 
us in the Adams Advice Letter. You provide some new information on this issue suggesting that 
private entities, such as PPYA, account for much of the Watermaster's funding. You then state that 
a majority of the Watermaster's funding comes from private entities. We note that this fact was not 
easily discernible from the information provided in either the original advice request or the present 
one. And because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact, we are unable to determine the 
accuracy of your conclusion. 
4 In any event, even assuming we found otherwise, this factor is less significant where the impetus for creation 
originates with the official action of a government agency, and not a statute or ordinance. (Sheeks Advice Letter, No. A-
90-026; Canni==o Advice Letter, No. A-97-562a.) 


File No. A-16-255 
Page No. 5 
To the extent your conclusion concerning the extent of the Watennaster's funding from 
private entities is premised on an accurate factual assessment, we are unable to conclude that the 
Watennaster is substantially funded by government entities. 
3. Is one of the princlpal pwposes for which the entity is formed to provide services or undertake 
obligations that public agencies are legally authorized to pe1form and which, in fact, they 
traditionally have pe1formed? 
In the Siegel opinion, this third criterion is a two-part inquiry that examines whether an 
entity perfonns a public function, and whether the service provided is one that is traditionally 
perfonned by public agencies. (Stark Advice Letter, No. A-03-015.) 
A. Public Function: 
We first look at factors considered by the Siegel opinion to be relevant to detennining 
whether an entity perfonns a public function. One such factor is the degree to which public agencies 
control or are involved in its operations. 
In our previous advice, we stated that 19 of the 28 total votes allotted to the nine voting 
members of the Watennaster are held by public agency representatives. We concluded therefore 
that the Watennaster serves a public function because it is controlled by governmental entity 
representatives. In response, you state that while it is true the Watermaster does have government 
entity voting members, the government entities represented have no authority themselves to make 
decisions with respect to apportioning groundwater rights. Furthennore, the governmental entities 
have no authority to make discretionary decisions concerning the groundwater rights because they 
are constrained by the Judgment. 
In the Siegel opinion, the Commission noted that .. further evidence that the Corporation is 
fulfilling a public function under this plan is that the water system is to be operated solely by city 
employees." In addition, the opinion looked at whether City Council members were members of the 
board of the nonprofit Corporation and considered the fact that the city council had a right to 
disapprove the name of anyone submitted to serve on the board. (111 re Siegel, supra.) 
In our prior advice, we looked to the degree of government involvement in the Watennaster 
and correctly concluded that because t 9 of the 28 allotted votes belonged to public agency 
representatives, it serves a public function. You raise, however, some valid points that differentiate 
this situation from the Siegel matter. For example, although there is significant government 
representation on the Watermaster committee, these representatives have no authority to apportion 
the groundwater rights among the entities. Those rights are set forth in the Judgment, which arose 
because of the lawsuit filed by a private entity and was crafted with involvement from private 
entities. Moreover, private entities have continued representation within the Watennaster and there 
is no indication that any public agency or public agency representative may disapprove any of the 
private entity representatives. 
We find that the Watermaster is performing a public-private function, with some 
involvement and input from public agency representatives. 


B. Service Traditionally Performed by Public Agencies: 
File No. A-16-255 
Page No. 6 
Secondly, we look at factors considered by the Siegel opinion to be relevant in detennining 
whether an entity perfonns a function that has traditionally been perfonned by public agencies. 
There, it was considered significant that the corporation in question provided a service commonly 
provided by municipalities in their public capacities, the operation of a water system. Therefore, the 
corporation in Siegel was clearly involved in a traditionally public activity - the operation and 
maintenance of a water system. 
You state public entities are not legally authorized to perform, nor have they traditionally 
perfonned, the functions undertaken by the Watennaster. Prior to the instant Judgment, no public 
agency involved in the litigation had the legal authority to adjudicate groundwater rights, apportion 
groundwater among the parties and to implement the physical solution. In contrast to Siegel, you 
claim that "apportioning groundwater rights and administering a public solution are acts that 
public agencies are expressly prohibited from engaging in." 
Again, we find some merit to your claims. Although there may be certain aspects of the 
Watennaster's functions that are ultimately beneficial to the public (e.g., ensuring that the 
Judgment's allocation of water to the municipalities involved is properly enforced), merely 
providing some public service does not mean that an entity will be found to be a governmental 
agency per se. (In re Leach (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 48.) There are other functions of the Watennaster 
(apportioning groundwater among the parties and implementing the physical solution) that do not 
appear to be functions traditionally or legally undertaken by public entities. As already mentioned, 
the presence of government entities in the underlying lawsuit was not a necessary factor to the 
fonnation of the Watermaster - it may be utilized in cases involving only private litigants. 
Under the facts presented, it appears that the Watennaster may be perfonning a semi-public 
function (with some input and involvement by government representatives), and it is providing a 
service that a public agency has not traditionally perfonned. Therefore, we are unable to conclude 
the third factor is met. 
4. Is the entity treated as a public entity by other statutolJ' provisions? 
The final factor that is considered in evaluating an entity's status under the Act is whether 
the entity is treated as a public entity by other provisions oflaw. We have previously stated that a 
requirement to follow the open meeting laws weighs in favor of the fourth criterion of the Siegel 
analysis. (Kranitz Advice Letter, No. A-03-204; Alperin Advice Letter, No. A-95-1 I 8.) 
In the Adams matter, we noted the Judgment requires that, to the extent practicable, the 
Watermaster conduct meetings in accordance with the provisions of the California Open Meetings 
Law (the "Brown Act"). We thus concluded that the requirement weighed in favor of the fourth 
criterion of the Siegel analysis. 
In response, you provided information showing that on three separate occasions the 
California Attorney General has opined that commissions and boards that provide counsel to the 
Court (similar to the Watermaster) are not subject to the Brown Act. Furthermore, you cite certain 
matters for the proposition that the "actions of a Watermaster and implementation of a physical 


File No. A-16-255 
Page No. 7 
solution in other adjudications have been held not to be the discretionary act of a public agency 
subject to CEQA." 
Looking to the requirement in the Judgment that it conduct meetings to tlze extent 
practicable in accordance with the Brown Act, you argue that the authority of the Watermaster 
(e.g., to establish regulations) is neither "conditioned upon" nor "subject to" compliance with the 
Brown Act. Instead, you allege the plain meaning of this language suggests the Watermaster will 
comply with the Brown Act whenever reasonable and sensible to do so. Unlike public agencies, 
however, the Watermaster is not bound to follow the Brown Act where it determines that it is not 
practicable. 
We believe that even though the Watermaster must conduct meetings in compliance with the 
Brown act where, as you suggest, reasonable and sensible to do so, the requirement to do so 
nonetheless exists. This is not the situation where the decision to comply with the Brown Act is a 
voluntary one. Rather, compliance must occur when practicable. Such requirement weighs in favor 
of the fourth criterion of the Siegel analysis. (Kranit7 Advice Letter, No. A-03-204; Alperi11 Advice 
Letter, No. A-95-118.) Therefore, this factor is met. 
However, we have advised that this factor alone is not determinative. (Epp Advice Letter, 
No. A-92-195.) Moreover, we have advised that meeting this factor is less significant where other 
statutory provisions applicable to public agencies treat the entity as a private entity. (Stone Advice 
Letter, No. A-97- 630.) You cited the example where the Watermaster is treated as a private entity 
pursuant to matters concerning CEQA; therefore, if your analysis is correct, we do not view this 
factor as significant to our analysis. 
Commission advice letters and an opinion state that it is not necessary that all four of the 
Siegel factors be satisfied for an entity to be considered a public agency. (In re Vonk ( 1981) 6 FPPC 
Ops. I; O'Shea Advice Letter, No. A-91-570.) It is only necessary that the entity satisfy enough of 
the four factors for its overall character to correspond to that of a government agency. (Rasia!, 
Advice Letter, No. A-01-020.) Therefore, the Siegel factors are not intended to be a definitive 
litmus test for determining whether an entity is public for purposes of the Act. Ultimately, the test 
must still be a factual analysis on a case-by-case basis. (111 re Vonk, supra.) 
In reassessing the Siegel factors in light of the new information you have provided, although 
the Watermaster may have some characteristics of a governmental agency, we are unable to 
conclude that it satisfies enough of the four factors to be considered a public agency the Act. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel

sections.question:
Under the Act, is the Six Basins Watennaster (the "Watennaster") a public agency required 
to adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code?
sections.conclusion:
Based on the new infonnation you have provided, we are unable to conclude that the 
Watennaster is a public agency under the Act, as discussed below.

sections.analysis:

funds is, a government agency;

the fundamental purpose of the "Siegel test" is to distinguish governmental from non-governmental agencies, without 
regard to more incidental characteristics.  
(3) Whether one of the principal purposes for which the entity was 
formed is to provide services or undertake obligations which public 
agencies are legally authorized to perform and which, in fact, they 
traditionally have performed; and 
(4) Whether the entity is treated as a public entity by other laws.

We apply the Siegel test to your situation taking into consideration the additional

representation on the Watermaster committee, these representatives have no authority to apportion 
the groundwater rights among the entities. Those rights are set forth in the Judgment, which arose 
because of the lawsuit filed by a private entity and was crafted with involvement from private 
entities. Moreover, private entities have continued representation within the Watennaster and there 
is no indication that any public agency or public agency representative may disapprove any of the 
private entity representatives. 
We find that the Watermaster is performing a public-private function, with some 
involvement and input from public agency representatives. 


B. Service Traditionally Performed by Public Agencies:  
Secondly, we look at factors considered by the Siegel opinion to be relevant in detennining 
whether an entity perfonns a function that has traditionally been perfonned by public agencies. 
There, it was considered significant that the corporation in question provided a service commonly 
provided by municipalities in their public capacities, the operation of a water system. Therefore, the 
corporation in Siegel was clearly involved in a traditionally public activity - the operation and 
maintenance of a water system. 
You state public entities are not legally authorized to perform, nor have they traditionally 
perfonned, the functions undertaken by the Watennaster. Prior to the instant Judgment, no public 
agency involved in the litigation had the legal authority to adjudicate groundwater rights, apportion 
groundwater among the parties and to implement the physical solution. In contrast to Siegel, you 
claim that "apportioning groundwater rights and administering a public solution are acts that 
public agencies are expressly prohibited from engaging in." 
Again, we find some merit to your claims. Although there may be certain aspects of the 
Watennaster's functions that are ultimately beneficial to the public (e.g., ensuring that the 
Judgment's allocation of water to the municipalities involved is properly enforced), merely 
providing some public service does not mean that an entity will be found to be a governmental 
agency per se. (In re Leach (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 48.) There are other functions of the Watennaster 
(apportioning groundwater among the parties and implementing the physical solution) that do not 
appear to be functions traditionally or legally undertaken by public entities. As already mentioned, 
the presence of government entities in the underlying lawsuit was not a necessary factor to the 
fonnation of the Watermaster - it may be utilized in cases involving only private litigants. 
Under the facts presented, it appears that the Watennaster may be perfonning a semi-public 
function (with some input and involvement by government representatives), and it is providing a 
service that a public agency has not traditionally perfonned. Therefore, we are unable to conclude 
the third factor is met. 
4. Is the entity treated as a public entity by other statutolJ' provisions? 
The final factor that is considered in evaluating an entity's status under the Act is whether 
the entity is treated as a public entity by other provisions oflaw. We have previously stated that a 
requirement to follow the open meeting laws weighs in favor of the fourth criterion of the Siegel 
analysis. (Kranitz Advice Letter, No. A-03-204; Alperin Advice Letter, No. A-95-1 I 8.) 
In the Adams matter, we noted the Judgment requires that, to the extent practicable, the 
Watermaster conduct meetings in accordance with the provisions of the California Open Meetings 
Law (the "Brown Act"). We thus concluded that the requirement weighed in favor of the fourth 
criterion of the Siegel analysis. 
In response, you provided information showing that on three separate occasions the 
California Attorney General has opined that commissions and boards that provide counsel to the 
Court (similar to the Watermaster) are not subject to the Brown Act. Furthermore, you cite certain 
matters for the proposition that the "actions of a Watermaster and implementation of a physical  
solution in other adjudications have been held not to be the discretionary act of a public agency 
subject to CEQA." 
Looking to the requirement in the Judgment that it conduct meetings to tlze extent 
practicable in accordance with the Brown Act, you argue that the authority of the Watermaster 
(e.g., to establish regulations) is neither "conditioned upon" nor "subject to" compliance with the 
Brown Act. Instead, you allege the plain meaning of this language suggests the Watermaster will 
comply with the Brown Act whenever reasonable and sensible to do so. Unlike public agencies, 
however, the Watermaster is not bound to follow the Brown Act where it determines that it is not 
practicable. 
We believe that even though the Watermaster must conduct meetings in compliance with the 
Brown act where, as you suggest, reasonable and sensible to do so, the requirement to do so 
nonetheless exists. This is not the situation where the decision to comply with the Brown Act is a 
voluntary one. Rather, compliance must occur when practicable. Such requirement weighs in favor 
of the fourth criterion of the Siegel analysis. (Kranit7 Advice Letter, No. A-03-204; Alperi11 Advice 
Letter, No. A-95-118.) Therefore, this factor is met. 
However, we have advised that this factor alone is not determinative. (Epp Advice Letter, 
No. A-92-195.) Moreover, we have advised that meeting this factor is less significant where other 
statutory provisions applicable to public agencies treat the entity as a private entity. (Stone Advice 
Letter, No. A-97- 630.) You cited the example where the Watermaster is treated as a private entity 
pursuant to matters concerning CEQA; therefore, if your analysis is correct, we do not view this 
factor as significant to our analysis. 
Commission advice letters and an opinion state that it is not necessary that all four of the 
Siegel factors be satisfied for an entity to be considered a public agency. (In re Vonk ( 1981) 6 FPPC 
Ops. I; O'Shea Advice Letter, No. A-91-570.) It is only necessary that the entity satisfy enough of 
the four factors for its overall character to correspond to that of a government agency. (Rasia!, 
Advice Letter, No. A-01-020.) Therefore, the Siegel factors are not intended to be a definitive 
litmus test for determining whether an entity is public for purposes of the Act. Ultimately, the test 
must still be a factual analysis on a case-by-case basis. (111 re Vonk, supra.) 
In reassessing the Siegel factors in light of the new information you have provided, although 
the Watermaster may have some characteristics of a governmental agency, we are unable to 
conclude that it satisfies enough of the four factors to be considered a public agency the Act.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Under the Act, is the Six Basins Watennaster (the "Watennaster") a public agency required 
to adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code?

CONCLUSION: Based on the new infonnation you have provided, we are unable to conclude that the 
Watennaster is a public agency under the Act, as discussed below.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION · 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 Lance H. Olson Olson Hagel & Fishburn 555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1425 Sacramento, CA 95814 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-255 Dear Mr. Olson: January 20, 2017 This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the disclosure and conflict of interest provisions of the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. (In re Oglesby (1975), 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTION Under the Act, is the Six Basins Watennaster (the "Watennaster") a public agency required to adopt and promulgate a conflict of interest code? CONCLUSION Based on the new infonnation you have provided, we are unable to conclude that the Watennaster is a public agency under the Act, as discussed below. FACTS On behalf the Pomona Valley Protective Association ("PVPA''), you request that we reconsider our advice and conclusion in the Adams Advice Letter, No. A-1 5-079.2 There, we examined the Watermaster utilizing the Siegel test and concluded that it was a public agency subject to the conflict of interest code requirements of the Act. PVPA is a member of the Watennaster, which was established by a final judgment (the "Judgment") entered in Southern California Water Company vs. City of la Verne, et al, a lawsuit 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are. to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. \ Because you ask that we reconsider the conclusion in the Adams Advice Letter, supra, we incorporate herein the facts and analysis from that letter. File No. A-16-255 Page No. 2 initiated in 1998 concerning groundwater adjudication of the hydrologic unit known as the Six Basins in eastern Los Angeles County. Following a period of extensive negotiation among the parties, the litigation was resolved pursuant to a stipulation among the public agencies and private litigants to allocate most of the groundwater in the defined area among the parties. It is customary in California groundwater adjudications to establish a Watermaster to administer and enforce a physical solution, an equitable remedy designed to implement the constitutional mandate and to maximize the beneficial use of water, subject to the ongoing supervision and jurisdiction of the Court. The Watermaster emanates from and enforces the judicial decree apportioning water among the parties. It assumes certain enumerated duties to implement the physical solution. The Judgment established a physical solution for the Six Basins Area and the Court expressly reserved jurisdiction over the implementation of the physical solution to address future or changed
